[Slide 13] CNNs III: When CNNs Fail — Failure Modes & Engineering Fixes
13
Case: Bearing Fault Detection
Problem: 0.5 mm crack in 500 mm raceway = 0.1% of image
CNN failure: Standard arch misses sub-pixel features
Fix: Multi-scale input + dilated conv (33×33 RF) + attention gating
Result: Detection rate 87% → 96%
Golden Rule
CNNs excel at pattern recognition but lack physical reasoning.
Safety-critical engineering requires: physics constraints, uncertainty quantification (Bayesian CNN), and human-in-the-loop validation (Grad-CAM). Never deploy CNN alone for safety-critical decisions.
Quick reference: Grid data → CNN  | Sequence data → 1D-CNN or Transformer  | Graph data → GNN  | Physics-rich → PINN hybrid

[Slide 19] GNNs II: Graph Taxonomy & Engineering Design Choices
19
Case: Digital Twin for Engine Health
Design Choices
Node features: Raw sensor readings, handcrafted features (kurtosis), or learned embeddings (1D-CNN).
Edge features: Physical distance, connection type (bolted/welded), or signal delay for causality.
Readout function: Global pooling → system health; node-level →component diagnosis; edge-level → connection integrity.
Depth control: 2–4 layers typical; deeper 𝖼 over-smoothing risk.
Graph: 200+ component nodes (12-ch sensors), edges via physical adjacency + thermal coupling + fluid path.
Architecture: 4-layer GraphSAGE + residual + edge-conditioned MP + GAT aggregation.
Outputs: Node health score, edge stress (connection integrity), graph-level cascade risk.

Parallelism Strategies: Scaling Across GPUs
Data Parallelism (DP)
Each GPU holds a full copy of the model; data is split across GPUs. Gradients are averaged after each step.
Simple · ✓ Scales well for moderate models · ✗ Model must fit in one GPU
Tensor Parallelism (TP)
Split individual weight matrices across GPUs. Each GPU computes part of each matmul.
Handles huge layers · ✗ High inter-GPU bandwidth required (NVLink)
Pipeline Parallelism (PP)
Split layers across GPUs (GPU 1 = layers 1–24, GPU 2 = layers 25–48, …). Data flows sequentially.
Low bandwidth needs · ✗ Pipeline bubbles reduce utilization

Causal Masking: Preventing the Future from
Leaking
Encoder vs. Decoder:
Encoder (BERT): Full attention — every token sees all others (bidirectional) Decoder (GPT): Causal mask — each token only sees past + self (autoregressive)
THE PROBLEM	MASKED ATTENTION MATRIX
During training, all tokens are processed in parallel. But for language generation, token t should only see tokens 1...t (not future tokens).
SOLUTION: CAUSAL (TRIANGULAR) MASK
Set attention scores to −∞ for positions j > i, so after softmax they become 0.
Gray cells = masked (set to -inf before softmax)
Why this enables parallelism
The mask makes causal attention a single masked matrix multiply — no sequential loop!
Training: fully parallel. Inference: sequential.

Hallucination: The Fundamental Limitation
WHAT IS HALLUCINATION?
The model generates text that is fluent, confident, and wrong. Not a bug — it's an inherent consequence of how LLMs work.
ROOT CAUSES
Statistical, not factual
The model predicts likely sequences, not verified facts. "Plausible" ≠ "true."
Training data noise
Internet data contains errors, contradictions, and outdated info. The model absorbs all of it.
Knowledge cutoff
No access to real-time information. "Invents" plausible answers for questions beyond training data.
Soft generalization
Neural networks interpolate — they can smoothly blend facts from different contexts, creating chimeric falsehoods.

MITIGATION STRATEGIES
RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation: ground outputs in retrieved documents
RLHF/alignment — train model to say "I don't know" rather than fabricate
Tool use — let model call calculators, search engines, databases
Self-consistency — sample multiple answers, check agreement
Engineering principle: Never trust LLM output for critical decisions without verification. Design systems with human-in-the-loop and factual grounding.

Key Takeaways
1
Text → Tokens → Embeddings → Transformer
The full pipeline: tokenize, embed, add positions, process through L transformer blocks, predict next token.
2
Attention = Soft database lookup (Q·K → weights → V)
Each token queries all others, scores relevance via dot product, and takes weighted average of values. O(T²) cost.
3
Multi-head = multiple perspectives, same cost
H heads with d/H dimensions each. GQA reduces KV cache by sharing K,V across query groups.
4
Decoder-only has won the architecture race
GPT, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA — all decoder-only with causal masking. Simple, scalable, general.
5
Scale creates surprising capabilities — and risks
Emergent abilities appear at scale. But so do hallucination and brittleness. Engineering safeguards are essential.
6
Foundation models are tools — treat them accordingly
Verify outputs, design for failure, use RAG and tools. The architecture is elegant; trust must be earned.

Constitutional AI: Self-Supervised Alignment
THE IDEA (ANTHROPIC, 2022)
Instead of relying solely on human feedback, use a set of principles (a "constitution") and have the model critique and revise its own outputs.
TWO-PHASE PROCESS
Phase 1: Critique & Revise (SL-CAI)
Model generates a (possibly harmful) response
Model critiques its own response using a principle
Model revises the response to comply
Fine-tune on revised outputs
Phase 2: RL-CAI
Use AI-generated preference labels (instead of human labels) to train a reward model, then run RLHF.
"Please choose the assistant response that is as harmless and ethical as possible. Do NOT choose responses that are toxic, racist, sexist, or socially biased."
Initial response (may be harmful)
Critique (using principle)
Revised response (aligned)
Repeat for many principles → diverse alignment
No human labelers needed for preference data!

[Slide 19] 19
The H3 Framework: Helpful, Harmless, Honest
Helpful
Follow user intent accurately; provide useful, complete answers; accomplish the task
Harmless
Refuse harmful requests; avoid toxic content; don't assist in dangerous activities
Honest
Acknowledge uncertainty; don't make up facts; calibrated confidence in answers
The tension: These goals can conflict. A model that is maximally harmless might refuse too many legitimate requests (under-helpful). Finding the right balance is the core challenge of alignment.
COMMON TENSION EXAMPLES
Real systems balance all three with tiered safety levels, context-dependent refusals, and calibrated uncertainty.

Choice depends on document type, accuracy needs, and compute budget
Overlap matters: 10–20% overlap between chunks prevents information loss at boundaries. Typical: 50-token overlap for 200-token chunks.
Rule of thumb: Start with fixed-size (200 tokens, 50 overlap). Upgrade to semantic only if retrieval precision is below target.

[Slide 24] 24
Common Failure Patterns
Four failure modes that degrade RAG system quality
Irrelevant Chunks Retrieved
Embedding model fails to capture domain-specific semantics. Query returns topically similar but factually irrelevant passages.
Impact: LLM generates answer from wrong context → hallucination
Lost-in-the-Middle
LLMs attend more to beginning and end of context. Relevant info in the middle of retrieved chunks gets ignored during generation.
Impact: Correct info retrieved but not used → incomplete answer
Chunk Boundary Splits
Key information spans two chunks. Neither chunk alone contains the complete answer. Fixed-size chunking is especially prone to this.
Impact: Partial context → truncated or wrong answer
Outdated Index
Source documents updated but vector index not re-indexed. Stale embeddings return old versions of documents with outdated information.
Impact: Factually correct retrieval of wrong version → stale answers

[Slide 26] Evaluation Metrics
Quantitative metrics for retrieval quality and answer relevance
Hit Rate @k
Fraction of queries where at least one relevant doc appears in top-k
Target: HR@5 ≥ 0.85
Mean Reciprocal Rank
Average of 1/rank of the first relevant result across all queries
Target: MRR ≥ 0.70
NDCG @k
Normalized discounted cumulative gain — rewards relevant docs ranked higher
Retrieval metrics (HR, MRR, NDCG) measure whether the right chunks are found. Evaluate with a labeled test set of 50–100 query-answer pairs.
End-to-end metrics: Use LLM-as-judge (GPT-4) to score answer faithfulness, relevance, and completeness on a 1–5 scale.

Advanced Topics & Future Directions

RAG is rapidly evolving — three frontier approaches
Self-Querying
LLM generates structured metadata filters from natural language queries. "Papers from 2024 about transformers" → year=2024 AND topic=transformers
Agentic RAG
RAG as a tool in an agent loop. Agent decides when to retrieve, refines queries iteratively, and combines results from multiple sources before answering.
Multi-modal RAG
Retrieve from images, tables, and text together. Vision-language models embed all modalities into shared vector space for unified search.
